core_meaning: <ruby>結構<rt>けっこう</rt></ruby> is a surprisingly versatile word that does three main jobs: (1) an adverb meaning 'quite / pretty / surprisingly' (more than expected), (2) a な-adjective meaning 'fine / satisfactory / good enough,' and (3) a polite refusal meaning 'no thank you / I'm fine' — the same form covers both acceptance and refusal depending on context, which trips up learners constantly.

sentences.jp: この<ruby>映画<rt>えいが</rt></ruby>、<ruby>結構<rt>けっこう</rt></ruby><ruby>面白<rt>おもしろ</rt></ruby>かったじゃん。
sentences.translation: That movie was actually pretty interesting, wasn't it.
sentences.notes: <ruby>結構<rt>けっこう</rt></ruby> as an adverb meaning 'quite / surprisingly' — it carries a light nuance of 'more than I expected.'

sentences.jp: A:「もう<ruby>少<rt>すこ</rt></ruby>しいかがですか？」B:「ありがとうございます、もう<ruby>結構<rt>けっこう</rt></ruby>です。」
sentences.translation: A: 'Would you like a little more?' B: 'Thank you, I'm quite fine / no thank you.'
sentences.notes: This is the famous ambiguous refusal use — context (a full plate, a satisfied tone) makes it clear it's a polite 'no,' not an enthusiastic 'yes.'

sentences.jp: <ruby>彼女<rt>かのじょ</rt></ruby>の<ruby>料理<rt>りょうり</rt></ruby>、<ruby>結構<rt>けっこう</rt></ruby><ruby>上手<rt>うま</rt></ruby>いんだよね。
sentences.translation: She's actually a pretty good cook, you know.
sentences.notes: Shows <ruby>結構<rt>けっこう</rt></ruby> modifying an い-adjective in a natural casual sentence with the softening んだよね ending.

sentences.jp: この<ruby>仕事<rt>しごと</rt></ruby>は<ruby>結構<rt>けっこう</rt></ruby><ruby>大変<rt>たいへん</rt></ruby>で、<ruby>毎日<rt>まいにち</rt></ruby><ruby>残業<rt>ざんぎょう</rt></ruby>しています。
sentences.translation: This job is quite tough — I work overtime every day.
sentences.notes: <ruby>結構<rt>けっこう</rt></ruby> here adds the nuance of 'more than you might think,' common in matter-of-fact descriptions of one's situation.

sentences.jp: A:「<ruby>手伝<rt>てつだ</rt></ruby>いましょうか？」B:「<ruby>結構<rt>けっこう</rt></ruby>です、<ruby>自分<rt>じぶん</rt></ruby>でできます。」
sentences.translation: A: 'Shall I help you?' B: 'That's fine, I can manage myself.'
sentences.notes: Another clear refusal use — when followed by a reason (<ruby>自分<rt>じぶん</rt></ruby>でできます), the 'no thank you' reading is unambiguous.

dont_use: Don't use <ruby>結構<rt>けっこう</rt></ruby>です on its own to mean 'yes please, that's great!' — without clear context it almost always reads as a polite refusal, and using it to accept something can confuse native speakers. Also avoid overusing it as a casual intensifier in very formal written contexts; <ruby>非常<rt>ひじょう</rt></ruby>に or かなり are more appropriate there. Finally, don't use <ruby>結構<rt>けっこう</rt></ruby> to describe something truly excellent or exceptional — it implies 'pretty good / satisfactory,' not 'outstanding,' so using it as high praise can accidentally undersell.

confused_with.contrast: Learners often swap <ruby>結構<rt>けっこう</rt></ruby> and かなり freely, but they have different flavors. かなり is a straightforward intensifier meaning 'considerably / fairly a lot' with no particular surprise nuance, while <ruby>結構<rt>けっこう</rt></ruby> often implies 'more than I expected' or 'surprisingly so.' Saying 「かなり<ruby>難<rt>むずか</rt></ruby>しかった」 states a fact neutrally, while 「<ruby>結構<rt>けっこう</rt></ruby><ruby>難<rt>むずか</rt></ruby>しかった」 carries a slight 'actually it was harder than I thought' nuance. Use <ruby>結構<rt>けっこう</rt></ruby> when you want to convey that slight surprise; use かなり when you simply want to say 'quite a lot' without that spin.

frequency: <ruby>結構<rt>けっこう</rt></ruby> is extremely common in everyday spoken Japanese across all age groups and registers. The adverb use (quite / pretty) appears constantly in casual conversation. The polite refusal use is essential for daily social situations. It's also natural in standard written Japanese, though formal documents would prefer <ruby>非常<rt>ひじょう</rt></ruby>に or かなり for the intensifier sense.
anki_hint: <ruby>結構<rt>けっこう</rt></ruby> wears three hats: 'pretty/quite' (adverb), 'fine/satisfactory' (な-adj), and 'no thank you' (polite refusal) — and the last two use the exact same phrase 「<ruby>結構<rt>けっこう</rt></ruby>です」, so always read the context!
